events.text: The patient is a 48-year-old female named Renata Kowalczyk. She has a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus managed with metformin, mild hypertension, and no prior hematologic conditions. She is a non-smoker, works as a high school biology teacher, and lives with her husband and two adult children. She has no known family history of leukemia or myelodysplastic syndrome. Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) performance status at presentation was 1.

At age 48 years, the patient was diagnosed with Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML), FAB M4 (Acute Myelomonocytic Leukemia), arising de novo. AJCC staging not applicable; SEER Summary Stage: Leukemia (not a solid tumor). Primary site: Bone marrow and peripheral blood (ICD-O-3 site code C42.1, Bone marrow). Histology: Acute myeloid leukemia, NOS (ICD-O-3 morphology code 9861/3). Presenting white blood cell count was 87,400/microliter (leukocytosis), hemoglobin 7.2 g/dL (anemia), platelet count 34,000/microliter (thrombocytopenia). Bone marrow biopsy showed 74% blasts. Peripheral smear demonstrated circulating blasts with Auer rods. Clinical presentation included a 3-week history of fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, spontaneous gingival bleeding, easy bruising, and night sweats. FLT3-ITD mutation identified with high allelic ratio (VAF 0.82, allelic ratio >0.5). NPM1 wild-type. CEBPA wild-type. IDH1/IDH2 wild-type. DNMT3A R882H co-mutation present. European LeukemiaNet (ELN) 2022 risk classification: Adverse (FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio without NPM1 mutation). Cytogenetics: Normal karyotype (46,XX) by conventional G-banding. Confirmation: Positive histology and flow cytometry. Diagnosis confirmed by bone marrow biopsy and aspirate.

At age 48 years, the patient had a bone marrow biopsy and aspirate pathology result. Specimen: Posterior iliac crest core biopsy and aspirate smears. Diagnosis: Acute myeloid leukemia (AML), FAB M4 morphology (acute myelomonocytic leukemia). Cellularity: Markedly hypercellular (>95%). Blast count: 74% by differential (>20% threshold met). Morphology: Medium to large blasts with high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio, folded/indented nuclei, prominent nucleoli; subset with monocytic differentiation showing abundant pale cytoplasm; rare Auer rods identified. Immunohistochemistry: CD34+, CD117+, CD33+, CD13+, MPO focally positive, CD64+, CD14 partially positive. Flow cytometry immunophenotype: CD45dim, CD34+, CD117+, HLA-DR+, CD33+, CD13+, CD14+/-, CD64+, MPO+, consistent with AML with monocytic differentiation. Cytochemistry: Non-specific esterase (NSE) positive in monocytic component. Iron stores: Decreased. Dysplasia: No significant multilineage dysplasia. Comment: Morphology, immunophenotype, and molecular findings are consistent with de novo AML (FAB M4). FLT3 ITD mutation confirmed by fragment analysis. DNMT3A R882H identified on reflex molecular testing.

At age 48 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing (NGS) performed for acute myeloid leukemia based on a specimen obtained from bone marrow aspirate at diagnosis. A comprehensive hematologic malignancy NGS panel (523-gene) was used. Actionable/clinically significant alterations: FLT3 internal tandem duplication (ITD) in exon 14-15 junction region, allelic ratio 0.82 (high allelic ratio, VAF 82%), insertion length 48 bp; DNMT3A p.R882H missense mutation (VAF 47%). Co-mutations and additional findings: TET2 wild-type; IDH1 wild-type; IDH2 wild-type; NPM1 wild-type; CEBPA wild-type; WT1 wild-type; RUNX1 wild-type; TP53 wild-type; NRAS wild-type; KRAS wild-type; PTPN11 wild-type; KIT wild-type; SF3B1 wild-type; ASXL1 wild-type; EZH2 wild-type; STAG2 wild-type. Copy number alterations: No pathogenic copy number alterations detected. Gene fusions: No AML-defining gene fusions detected (PML-RARA, RUNX1-RUNX1T1, CBFB-MYH11, DEK-NUP214, BCR-ABL1 all negative by RT-PCR reflex). FLT3 point mutation (D835): Wild-type. KIT D816: Wild-type. Cytogenetics (conventional karyotype): 46,XX [20/20] - normal female karyotype. FISH panel: Negative for t(8;21), inv(16), t(15;17), t(9;22), MLL/KMT2A rearrangement, EVI1 rearrangement. Tumor mutational burden: 3 mut/Mb (low). Microsatellite instability: MSS. ELN 2022 molecular risk: Adverse (FLT3-ITD high AR, NPM1 wild-type). Summary: Molecularly defined AML with FLT3-ITD (high AR) and DNMT3A R882H co-mutation, normal karyotype.

At age 48 years, the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis (with contrast) performed at initial AML workup. Findings: Mild splenomegaly with spleen measuring 14.2 cm in craniocaudal dimension (normal <13 cm). No hepatomegaly. No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes by size criteria. No mediastinal masses or adenopathy. Lungs: No consolidation, effusion, or nodules. No lytic or blastic osseous lesions. No soft tissue masses. Impression: Mild splenomegaly, likely related to known AML diagnosis. No evidence of extramedullary leukemic disease (granulocytic sarcoma) by CT imaging. No occult infection identified.

At age 48 years, the patient had a cardiac echocardiogram (transthoracic) performed as pre-chemotherapy cardiac assessment. Findings: Left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) 62% (normal). No wall motion abnormalities. No valvular disease. No pericardial effusion. Normal diastolic function. Left ventricular end-diastolic diameter: 4.9 cm (normal). Right heart: Normal right ventricular size and function. Pulmonary artery systolic pressure estimated at 28 mmHg (normal). Impression: Normal biventricular function. Adequate cardiac reserve for planned anthracycline-based induction chemotherapy.

At age 48 years, the patient was admitted to the inpatient hematology unit and received curative-intent induction chemotherapy with the 7+3 regimen plus midostaurin (standard of care for FLT3-mutant AML per RATIFY trial). Treatment details: Cytarabine 200 mg/m2/day continuous IV infusion on days 1-7; Daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 IV on days 1, 2, and 3; Midostaurin 50 mg orally twice daily on days 8-21 (FLT3 inhibitor, withheld during cytotoxic phase per protocol). Prophylactic antimicrobials: Levofloxacin, fluconazole, acyclovir. Allopurinol and aggressive IV hydration for tumor lysis prophylaxis. GCSF (filgrastim) deferred until count recovery. Central venous access: Peripherally inserted central catheter (PICC) placed on day 0. Blood product support: Packed red blood cell transfusions (4 units) and platelet transfusions (8 pooled units) during induction neutropenia. Dose reductions: None. Cycle 1 of planned induction initiated.

At age 48 years (during induction, day 10), the patient developed febrile neutropenia (absolute neutrophil count 0/microliter, temperature 38.7 degrees C). Blood cultures drawn; empirical IV piperacillin-tazobactam initiated. Grade 3 mucositis requiring IV analgesics and parenteral nutrition. Grade 2 nausea/vomiting managed with ondansetron and lorazepam. Blood cultures subsequently grew Escherichia coli (pan-sensitive); antibiotics narrowed to IV ceftriaxone with clinical improvement. Duration of neutropenia: 22 days from start of chemotherapy. Grade 1 transaminase elevation (ALT 68 U/L, AST 52 U/L), resolved spontaneously. No cardiac toxicity; repeat LVEF not indicated at this early timepoint. Midostaurin briefly held for 3 days during peak mucositis due to poor oral intake, then resumed. Patient recovered from all acute toxicities prior to day 28 bone marrow assessment.

At age 48 years (day 28 post-induction), the patient had a bone marrow biopsy and aspirate performed to assess treatment response. Specimen: Posterior iliac crest core biopsy and aspirate. Cellularity: Moderately hypocellular (30-40%), consistent with post-chemotherapy aplasia/early recovery. Blast count: 2% by morphologic differential count (below 5% threshold). Residual AML: Not detected by morphology. Flow cytometry (high-sensitivity, 8-color panel): No immunophenotypically aberrant myeloid blasts detected (sensitivity 0.01%); MRD negative. FLT3-ITD by PCR: Undetectable (sensitivity 10^-4). DNMT3A R882H by ddPCR: VAF 1.8% (low-level residual, consistent with clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential [CHIP] rather than residual AML; may represent pre-existing CHIP clone). Cytogenetics: 46,XX [20/20] - normal karyotype restored. Conclusion: Complete morphologic remission (CR) achieved after first induction. MRD negative by multiparameter flow cytometry. Adequate early hematopoietic reconstitution in progress.

At age 48 years (approximately 5 weeks post-induction start), the patient had an oncology clinic visit for day 35 assessment. Cancer status: Complete remission (CR), MRD negative by flow cytometry and FLT3-ITD PCR. Patient performance status improved to ECOG 0. Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL (recovering), WBC 3,200/microliter, ANC 1,800/microliter, platelets 98,000/microliter (recovering). Gingival bleeding fully resolved. No bleeding events during hospitalization beyond minor oozing at PICC site. Weight: 64 kg (baseline 67 kg; 4.5% weight loss during induction, partially recovered). Patient tolerating oral midostaurin maintenance well (no nausea). Discussed ELN Adverse risk classification and strong recommendation for allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (alloHSCT) in first CR given FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio. HLA typing sent. Sibling HLA typing sent (two siblings). Referral made to the transplant program. Patient and family counseled extensively on transplant rationale, risks, and alternatives. Patient expressed understanding and agreement to pursue transplant. Decision: Proceed with HLA typing and transplant evaluation; hold consolidation chemotherapy pending matched donor identification.

At age 48 years (pre-transplant workup, approximately 7 weeks after diagnosis), the patient had a PET-CT scan (18F-FDG) performed as part of transplant evaluation to rule out extramedullary disease. Findings: No FDG-avid foci to suggest active leukemic disease or granulocytic sarcoma. Splenomegaly resolved; spleen now measures 11.8 cm (normal). Mild residual hypermetabolism in posterior iliac crests bilaterally, consistent with bone marrow recovery (expected post-chemotherapy finding). No mediastinal, hilar, axillary, retroperitoneal, or inguinal adenopathy. No hepatic or splenic lesions. No osseous lesions. Impression: No evidence of extramedullary AML. Findings consistent with CR status. Cleared for allogeneic transplant from metabolic imaging standpoint.

At age 48 years (transplant evaluation, approximately 8 weeks after diagnosis), HLA typing results returned. Patient HLA type: A*02:01, A*24:02; B*07:02, B*35:01; C*07:02, C*04:01; DRB1*11:01, DRB1*15:01; DQB1*03:01, DQB1*06:02 (high-resolution 10/10 allele typing). Sibling HLA results: Neither sibling is a 10/10 HLA match (one sibling is a 7/10 mismatch). Unrelated donor search initiated through the National Marrow Donor Program (NMDP/Be The Match). A 10/10 allele-level matched unrelated donor (MUD) identified within 3 weeks of search initiation; donor is a 38-year-old CMV-negative male. CMV serostatus of patient: CMV IgG positive (CMV+). Donor/recipient CMV pairing: CMV+/- (recipient positive, donor negative) - noted as intermediate risk for CMV reactivation post-transplant. Transplant evaluation complete: Cardiac clearance confirmed (LVEF 62%); pulmonary function tests: FEV1 88% predicted, DLCO 82% predicted (adequate); dental clearance obtained; ophthalmology clearance obtained; nephrology: eGFR 72 mL/min/1.73m2 (adequate). ECOG PS: 0. Transplant program accepted patient. Conditioning regimen planned: Fludarabine/busulfan (FluBu4) myeloablative conditioning. GVHD prophylaxis planned: Tacrolimus/methotrexate.

At age 48 years (approximately 10 weeks after initial AML diagnosis), the patient received one cycle of bridging consolidation chemotherapy with intermediate-dose cytarabine (IDAC) prior to transplant to maintain remission during the donor workup period and reduce relapse risk. Treatment: Cytarabine 1.5 g/m2 IV every 12 hours on days 1, 3, and 5 (6 doses total). Midostaurin 50 mg twice daily continued days 8-21. Prophylactic antimicrobials continued. Blood product support as needed. Rationale: Bridging consolidation to maintain CR and reduce MRD during the approximately 4-week pre-transplant interval. Patient tolerated therapy with manageable toxicity (Grade 2 ocular toxicity - bilateral conjunctivitis requiring prophylactic steroid eye drops as per cytarabine high-dose eye drop protocol). ANC nadir day 12; recovery by day 20. Post-consolidation bone marrow: Not repeated given recent MRD-negative assessment and no clinical signs of relapse.

At age 48 years (during IDAC consolidation, approximately day 8 of consolidation cycle), the patient developed cytarabine-associated bilateral conjunctivitis (grade 2 ocular toxicity, expected with cytarabine use). Managed with prophylactic steroid ophthalmic drops (prednisolone 1% drops four times daily bilaterally) starting on day 3 of cytarabine per institutional protocol. Symptoms: bilateral eye redness, photophobia, and tearing. No corneal changes on slit-lamp exam. Symptoms resolved by day 18. Grade 1 peripheral neuropathy noted (bilateral hand tingling), attributed to cytarabine; monitoring ongoing. No cerebellar toxicity. Febrile neutropenia did not recur during this cycle.

At age 48 years (approximately 14 weeks after initial AML diagnosis), the patient was admitted for myeloablative allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (alloHSCT) from a 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated donor (MUD). Conditioning regimen: Fludarabine 40 mg/m2/day IV on days -6 through -3 (4 days); Busulfan 3.2 mg/kg/day IV on days -6 through -3 (4 days, FluBu4 myeloablative conditioning). Busulfan pharmacokinetic (PK) monitoring performed; doses adjusted to achieve target AUC 4500-6000 micromol*min/L. GVHD prophylaxis: Tacrolimus IV starting day -3 (target trough 8-12 ng/mL), transitioned to oral; Short-course methotrexate 15 mg/m2 IV on day +1, 10 mg/m2 IV on days +3, +6, and +11. Anti-thymocyte globulin (ATG, rabbit, thymoglobulin) 2.5 mg/kg/day IV on days -3, -2, -1 (total 7.5 mg/kg) for additional GVHD prophylaxis and engraftment facilitation in MUD setting. Stem cell source: Peripheral blood stem cells (PBSC); donor G-CSF mobilized. CD34+ cell dose infused: 6.8 x 10^6 CD34+ cells/kg (adequate). Day 0: Stem cell infusion uneventful, no infusion reactions.

At age 48 years (post-transplant, approximately day +7 to +18), the patient experienced expected transplant-related toxicities. Grade 3 mucositis: Oral/gastrointestinal mucositis (likely exacerbated by methotrexate GVHD prophylaxis), managed with IV morphine PCA, IV fluids, and total parenteral nutrition for 8 days. Grade 2 nausea/vomiting, managed with ondansetron, lorazepam, and dexamethasone. Febrile neutropenia at day +8 (ANC 0/microliter, temperature 39.1 degrees C): Blood cultures negative; empirical IV meropenem initiated with clinical response; GCSF (filgrastim) held per institutional transplant protocol. Sinusoidal obstruction syndrome (SOS/VOD) monitoring: Bilirubin peak 2.8 mg/dL (day +12), ALT 142 U/L; met Modified Seattle Criteria for mild SOS, managed with fluid restriction and weight monitoring; resolved by day +20 without defibrotide. CMV surveillance by quantitative PCR weekly from day +14: CMV viremia detected at day +28 (460 IU/mL); pre-emptive treatment with valganciclovir initiated. No BK virus hemorrhagic cystitis. No thrombotic microangiopathy.

At age 48 years (day +30 post-transplant assessment), the patient had a transplant clinic visit for engraftment assessment. Neutrophil engraftment: Day +14 (ANC >500/microliter for 3 consecutive days). Platelet engraftment: Day +22 (platelets >50,000/microliter unsupported). CBC at day +30: WBC 4,200/microliter, ANC 3,100/microliter, hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL, platelets 112,000/microliter - demonstrating full count recovery. Chimerism analysis (peripheral blood, day +28): 99% donor chimerism by short tandem repeat (STR) analysis; <1% residual host cells detected (essentially complete donor engraftment). CMV viremia: 460 IU/mL on day +28, declining to 210 IU/mL at day +35 on valganciclovir; continued monitoring. Tacrolimus trough: 9.2 ng/mL (within target range). No clinical signs of acute GVHD (Grade 0). Skin: No rash. Liver: Bilirubin normalized to 0.9 mg/dL. GI: No diarrhea. Patient ECOG PS 1 (mild fatigue). Midostaurin not restarted post-transplant (per institutional protocol; FLT3 inhibitor maintenance post-alloHSCT not standard at time of this treatment). Plan: Continue tacrolimus taper per schedule starting day +90; continue valganciclovir; weekly CBC, CMP, tacrolimus levels; monthly chimerism.

At age 48 years (day +60 post-transplant), the patient had a bone marrow biopsy and aspirate performed for disease restaging. Specimen: Posterior iliac crest core biopsy and aspirate smears. Cellularity: Moderately cellular (55-65%), appropriate for 60 days post-transplant. Morphology: Trilineage hematopoiesis present; maturing myeloid, erythroid, and megakaryocytic elements without dysplasia. No morphologic evidence of residual or recurrent AML. Blast count: <2% by differential (within normal limits). Flow cytometry MRD: No immunophenotypically aberrant myeloid blasts detected (sensitivity 0.01%); MRD negative. FLT3-ITD PCR: Undetectable. DNMT3A R882H by ddPCR: VAF 2.1% (stable low-level, consistent with pre-existing CHIP in context of now-donor hematopoiesis - note: this low-level signal likely represents persistence of CHIP within residual host cells given 99% donor chimerism). Chimerism on sorted CD34+ progenitors: 100% donor. Cytogenetics: 46,XY [20/20] - male karyotype confirmed, consistent with male donor engraftment. Conclusion: Sustained complete remission, MRD negative, complete donor engraftment confirmed by XY karyotype.

At age 49 years (approximately day +90 post-transplant), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis (with contrast) for transplant follow-up surveillance. Findings: No splenomegaly (spleen 10.2 cm). No hepatomegaly. No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes. Lungs: No consolidation, nodules, or pleural effusions. No evidence of pulmonary fungal infection. No osseous lesions suggestive of extramedullary leukemia. Abdomen/pelvis: Normal liver, no biliary dilation. Kidneys normal. No free fluid. Incidental finding: Small uterine fibroid (2.1 cm, intramural), unchanged from prior exam; clinically insignificant. Impression: No evidence of extramedullary leukemic relapse or opportunistic infection. Normal post-transplant imaging.

At age 49 years (day +100 post-transplant clinic visit), the patient had a transplant follow-up assessment. CBC: WBC 5,800/microliter, ANC 3,900/microliter, Hgb 11.4 g/dL, platelets 148,000/microliter (near-normal counts). Tacrolimus taper initiated per protocol; trough now 7.4 ng/mL. CMV surveillance: CMV PCR undetectable at day +60 and +90; valganciclovir discontinued after 2 negative PCRs (day +70 and +84). Acute GVHD: None observed at any point post-transplant (Grade 0 acute GVHD). New findings: Patient reporting dry eyes, mild dry mouth (xerostomia), and a subtle skin tightening over both forearms noted on exam. Skin findings: Fine, reticulated erythematous patches over both forearms with early lichenoid features, no bullae or ulceration. These findings are consistent with early/mild chronic GVHD (cGVHD) involving skin and sicca syndrome. NIH consensus criteria staging: Mild chronic GVHD (skin score 1, mouth score 1, eye score 1; total involvement: 3 organs, all mild; overall NIH global score: Mild). Plan: Initiate topical management; defer systemic immunosuppression given mild NIH score; continue tacrolimus at current dose; add topical tacrolimus 0.1% ointment to affected skin areas twice daily; lubricating eye drops (preservative-free artificial tears); biotene oral rinse for xerostomia; ophthalmology referral; dermatology referral; patient educated on sun protection and skin hygiene.

At age 49 years (day +100 to +180 post-transplant period), the patient experienced ongoing mild chronic GVHD affecting skin (lichenoid plaques on forearms and shins, BSA <10%), eyes (dry eye syndrome, requiring lubricating drops 4-6 times daily; Schirmer test 7 mm/5 min), and oral mucosa (xerostomia, mild oral sensitivity). NIH global severity: Mild. Management: Topical tacrolimus 0.1% ointment to skin lesions; clobetasol 0.05% ointment for more symptomatic areas; preservative-free artificial tears and lubricating ophthalmic gel; cyclosporine 0.05% ophthalmic emulsion initiated by ophthalmology for dry eye; biotene products for oral dryness; dental care every 6 months due to increased caries risk. Systemic corticosteroids: Not required (mild cGVHD). Tacrolimus CNI continued as single-agent systemic immunosuppression, maintained at trough 5-8 ng/mL to balance cGVHD control against infection risk. Prophylactic medications: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (PCP prophylaxis), acyclovir (HSV/VZV prophylaxis), continued while on immunosuppression. No major infectious complications during this period.

At age 49 years (day +180 post-transplant), the patient had a 6-month transplant follow-up visit. Disease status: Sustained complete remission; MRD negative by most recent bone marrow evaluation (day +60). Chimerism (peripheral blood, day +150): 100% donor chimerism. CBC: WBC 6,200/microliter, ANC 4,100/microliter, Hgb 12.6 g/dL, platelets 176,000/microliter (normal). Tacrolimus trough: 6.1 ng/mL; continuing slow taper per protocol. Chronic GVHD status: Mild, stable; skin lesions improved significantly with topical therapies (now only faint post-inflammatory changes); oral symptoms improving; eyes remain dry but better controlled on cyclosporine drops. No new organ involvement. Functional status: ECOG PS 0; patient returned to teaching duties part-time at month 4, full-time at month 5. Vaccinations: Inactivated influenza vaccine administered (immunization program initiated per transplant schedule). PCP prophylaxis continued. Acyclovir prophylaxis continued. Type 2 diabetes management: Metformin resumed at day +90 after renal function confirmed stable; HbA1c 7.2% (adequate control in setting of tacrolimus-related insulin resistance). Plan: Continue tacrolimus taper; repeat bone marrow biopsy at day +365; ongoing surveillance for relapse, GVHD, and late effects.

At age 49 years (day +270 post-transplant), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed for annual surveillance. Findings: No evidence of AML relapse or extramedullary disease. No new lymphadenopathy. Lungs: No nodules, consolidation, or pleural effusion. Trace bilateral pleural thickening, likely related to prior infection/treatment; no change from prior. No osseous lesions. Spleen: Normal size (9.8 cm). Liver: Normal; no focal lesions. Incidental: The previously noted uterine fibroid is stable (2.2 cm). Impression: No radiographic evidence of AML relapse. No new findings of clinical concern. Continued surveillance recommended per transplant protocol.

At age 49 years (day +365, one year post-transplant), the patient had a bone marrow biopsy and aspirate for the 1-year post-transplant disease reassessment. Specimen: Posterior iliac crest core biopsy and aspirate. Cellularity: Normocellular (40-50%), appropriate for age and post-transplant status. Morphology: Normal trilineage hematopoiesis. No morphologic evidence of AML or myelodysplasia. Blast count: <2% (within normal limits). Flow cytometry MRD: Negative (sensitivity 0.01%); no immunophenotypically aberrant blasts. FLT3-ITD PCR: Undetectable. DNMT3A R882H ddPCR: Not detected (below assay limit of detection). Cytogenetics: 46,XY [20/20] - continued complete male donor engraftment. Chimerism (peripheral blood and bone marrow CD34+): 100% donor. Conclusion: Sustained complete remission at 1 year post-alloHSCT. MRD negative. Complete donor engraftment. Findings are highly favorable and consistent with cure in the context of FLT3-ITD AML.

At age 49 years (day +365, one-year post-transplant milestone visit), the patient had a comprehensive survivorship assessment. Disease status: Sustained complete morphologic and molecular remission. MRD negative. 100% donor chimerism. Overall assessment: Excellent response with disease-free survival of 12 months from transplant (approximately 14 months from initial AML diagnosis). Chronic GVHD status: Mild cGVHD, resolving - skin lichenoid changes nearly completely resolved with topical therapies; dry eye symptoms improving; xerostomia much improved. NIH global score revised to minimal/resolving. Tacrolimus taper plan: Plan to complete taper by month 18 post-transplant if cGVHD remains stable. Immunosuppression: Tacrolimus trough now 3.8 ng/mL (nearing discontinuation threshold). Immunization update: Vaccinations updated per 1-year post-transplant schedule (pneumococcal, meningococcal, diphtheria-tetanus, inactivated polio). Metabolic/late effects monitoring: Bone density DXA performed (Z-score -1.1, borderline low; calcium and vitamin D supplementation initiated); ophthalmology: cataract monitoring (mild posterior subcapsular cataract developing in left eye, attributed to prior steroid use; monitoring); endocrinology: ovarian function testing - FSH elevated at 87 mIU/mL, AMH undetectable, consistent with chemotherapy/conditioning-induced premature ovarian insufficiency; referred to reproductive endocrinology for hormone replacement therapy discussion. Thyroid function: Normal TSH. Renal function: eGFR 64 mL/min/1.73m2 (mild reduction from baseline, stable; likely multifactorial including tacrolimus nephrotoxicity). Diabetes management: HbA1c 7.0%, metformin dose optimized. Patient quality of life: Very good; patient reports feeling well and is fully active. Discussed continued annual surveillance plan, late effects monitoring, and signs and symptoms of relapse to watch for.

At age 50 years (approximately day +540, 18 months post-transplant), the patient had a survivorship clinic follow-up. Disease status: Continued complete remission; no evidence of AML relapse. Most recent bone marrow (day +365) MRD negative. Chronic GVHD: Resolved - no active skin, eye, or oral GVHD manifestations. Tacrolimus: Successfully discontinued at month 18 (today); patient transitioned off all systemic immunosuppression. Infectious prophylaxis: PCP prophylaxis (TMP-SMX) and acyclovir discontinued following tacrolimus discontinuation. CBC: WBC 7,100/microliter, ANC 4,800/microliter, Hgb 13.1 g/dL, platelets 204,000/microliter (fully normal). Renal function: eGFR 70 mL/min/1.73m2 (improved after tacrolimus dose reduction/discontinuation). Bone health: Repeat DXA (18 months): Improvement in bone density Z-score to -0.8 on calcium/vitamin D supplementation; no bisphosphonate needed at this time. Premature ovarian insufficiency: Patient initiated low-dose hormone replacement therapy (HRT) with transdermal estradiol and oral progesterone under guidance of reproductive endocrinology; significant improvement in vasomotor symptoms and quality of life. Oncology assessment: Next surveillance bone marrow biopsy planned at 2 years post-transplant (day +730). Counseling provided on late relapse risk (low but not zero), secondary malignancy surveillance, and healthy lifestyle measures. Patient remains very motivated and grateful; fully reintegrated into professional and family life. Overall clinical impression: Excellent long-term outcome in high-risk AML (ELN Adverse) following alloHSCT in first CR.

documents.text:
WHOLE-BODY PET-CT WITH 18F-FDG
PRE-ALLOGENEIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANT EVALUATION

PATIENT: Renata Kowalczyk
INDICATION: 48-year-old female with newly diagnosed acute myeloid leukemia (AML), FAB M4, FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio. Status post 7+3 induction chemotherapy (cytarabine/daunorubicin) plus midostaurin; day 28 bone marrow biopsy confirmed complete morphologic remission with MRD negativity by multiparameter flow cytometry. Referred for whole-body 18F-FDG PET-CT as part of pre-allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant (alloHSCT) evaluation. Clinical question: Rule out occult extramedullary AML (granulocytic/myeloid sarcoma) prior to planned myeloablative conditioning.

COMPARISON: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast performed at initial AML diagnosis (approximately 7 weeks prior).

TECHNIQUE: Whole-body 18F-FDG PET-CT acquired from skull base to mid-thigh using a combined PET-CT scanner. The patient fasted for a minimum of 6 hours prior to FDG administration. Serum glucose at time of injection: 104 mg/dL (within acceptable range for diagnostic quality). FDG dose administered intravenously: 10.2 mCi (377 MBq). Uptake phase: 62 minutes in a quiet, temperature-controlled environment. PET images were acquired in 3D mode with CT-based attenuation correction. CT component performed with IV contrast (100 mL iohexol 350 mg I/mL; no adverse reactions). CT technique: 120 kVp, automated mA modulation; slice thickness 3 mm (fused), 1.25 mm (diagnostic CT). PET data reconstructed using iterative reconstruction with time-of-flight and point spread function modeling. Quantitative SUV measurements performed on areas of interest using lean body mass correction (SUVbw and SUVlbm reported).

---

FINDINGS:

HEAD AND NECK:
The brain demonstrates the expected pattern of diffuse cortical and basal ganglia FDG uptake, physiologically symmetric. No focal intracranial areas of abnormal hypermetabolism. No leptomeningeal or dural-based FDG-avid lesions. Parotid and submandibular salivary glands show symmetric physiologic uptake. Oral mucosa demonstrates mildly increased diffuse activity, consistent with post-chemotherapy mucositis changes (clinical history of grade 3 mucositis approximately 4 weeks prior). Cervical lymph nodes: Scattered submandibular and upper cervical nodes are at the lower limits of normal in size (largest 7 mm short axis) with no discernible FDG activity above background. Neck soft tissues otherwise unremarkable. Thyroid gland: No focal uptake; no nodules or masses identified on CT.

THORAX:
Mediastinum: No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes. Prevascular, paratracheal, subcarinal, and paraesophageal nodal stations are unremarkable. No discrete FDG-avid mediastinal mass or nodal aggregates. No anterior mediastinal lesion to suggest granulocytic sarcoma at this site. Trachea and central airways patent. Esophagus: No mural thickening or abnormal FDG activity.

Hila: Hilar soft tissues are symmetric and within normal limits bilaterally. No hilar adenopathy. No abnormal hilar FDG uptake.

Lungs: Lung parenchyma appears clear bilaterally. No discrete nodules, consolidative opacities, or ground-glass change. No pleural effusions. No pleural or pericardial FDG-avid lesions. No evidence of fungal or other opportunistic pulmonary infection by CT morphology. Lung windows reviewed carefully for nodules -- none identified down to 4 mm. The previously documented clear lung fields at initial staging CT are maintained.

Axillae: No enlarged or FDG-avid axillary lymph nodes bilaterally. Scattered small axillary nodes (up to 9 mm) without increased metabolic activity are noted, consistent with reactive or normal nodes.

Heart and Pericardium: Normal cardiac chamber sizes. No pericardial effusion. Physiologic FDG activity in myocardium (variable, patchy; not interpretable as pathologic). No FDG-avid pericardial or epicardial lesion.

Breast tissue: Unremarkable bilaterally. No focal mass or abnormal uptake.

ABDOMEN:
Liver: The liver is normal in size (craniocaudal span approximately 14.5 cm in midclavicular line). Parenchymal density is homogeneous on CT without focal hypo- or hyperdense lesions. CHIEF COMPLAINT
Ms. Renata Kowalczyk is a 48-year-old female with newly diagnosed, de novo acute myeloid leukemia (AML), FAB M4 subtype, FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, ELN 2022 Adverse risk, who presents today for review of HLA typing results, donor search update, and completion of pre-transplant evaluation in preparation for planned allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (alloHSCT) in first complete remission (CR1).

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Kowalczyk is a 48-year-old high school biology teacher who presented approximately 8 weeks ago with a 3-week history of progressive fatigue, exertional dyspnea, spontaneous gingival bleeding, easy bruising, and drenching night sweats. She was found to have marked leukocytosis (WBC 87,400/mcL), severe anemia (hemoglobin 7.2 g/dL), and thrombocytopenia (platelets 34,000/mcL). Bone marrow biopsy confirmed AML, FAB M4, with 74% blasts and circulating blasts with Auer rods on peripheral smear. Comprehensive molecular workup by 523-gene NGS panel identified FLT3-ITD with a high allelic ratio of 0.82 (VAF 82%, insertion length 48 bp) and DNMT3A R882H co-mutation (VAF 47%). NPM1, IDH1, IDH2, CEBPA, TP53, RUNX1, and all other prognostically relevant genes were wild-type. Cytogenetics showed a normal female karyotype (46,XX). She was classified as ELN 2022 Adverse risk based on FLT3-ITD with high allelic ratio in the absence of NPM1 mutation.

She was admitted and received standard induction chemotherapy with the 7+3 regimen (cytarabine 200 mg/m2/day continuous infusion days 1–7; daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 IV on days 1, 2, and 3) plus midostaurin 50 mg orally twice daily on days 8–21, per the RATIFY trial protocol for FLT3-mutant AML. Induction was complicated by febrile neutropenia with E. coli bacteremia (pan-sensitive, treated with narrowed antibiotics after cultures), Grade 3 mucositis requiring IV morphine and parenteral nutrition for several days, and Grade 2 nausea/vomiting. Neutropenia lasted 22 days. Midostaurin was held briefly during peak mucositis and then resumed. She recovered from all acute toxicities prior to the day 28 marrow assessment.

Day 28 bone marrow biopsy confirmed complete morphologic remission (CR1): blast count 2%, no morphologically aberrant blasts, MRD-negative by multiparameter flow cytometry (sensitivity 0.01%), and FLT3-ITD undetectable by PCR (sensitivity 10^-4). DNMT3A R882H persisted at VAF 1.8% by ddPCR, felt to represent pre-existing clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential (CHIP) rather than residual disease given the MRD-negative status by flow and FLT3-ITD PCR, and the known association of DNMT3A R882H with CHIP. Cytogenetics restored to 46,XX.

At her day 35 clinic visit, the decision was made to pursue allogeneic transplant in CR1 given her adverse-risk disease features. This recommendation is strongly supported by European LeukemiaNet guidance and multiple large retrospective and prospective analyses demonstrating that alloHSCT in CR1 provides a substantial relapse-free and overall survival benefit for patients with FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio AML compared to chemotherapy alone, even with midostaurin maintenance. HLA typing was sent for the patient and her two siblings at that visit, and an unrelated donor search was initiated simultaneously through NMDP/Be The Match as a contingency, given the estimated 25% per-sibling probability of HLA match.

Subsequently, a PET-CT scan was performed as part of transplant workup. This showed resolution of the splenomegaly (spleen now 11.8 cm, down from 14.2 cm at diagnosis), no FDG-avid foci to suggest extramedullary leukemia or granulocytic sarcoma, and mild residual bilateral iliac crest hypermetabolism consistent with ongoing marrow recovery. The metabolic imaging was reassuring and cleared her for proceeding with transplant from an extramedullary disease standpoint.

Today we are reviewing the complete HLA typing results and finalizing the transplant plan.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Kowalczyk is a 48-year-old woman with de novo AML, FAB M4, harboring FLT3-ITD at a high allelic ratio (AR 0.82) and DNMT3A R882H, ELN 2022 Adverse-risk classification, who has achieved complete morphologic and molecular remission (CR1, MRD-negative) after a single cycle of 7+3 plus midostaurin induction. She has now completed full pre-transplant evaluation and a 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated donor has been identified. She is cleared for proceeding with allogeneic HSCT.

1. ALLOGENEIC HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION — PLANNED IN CR1:
   - INDICATION: Adverse-risk AML (FLT3-ITD high AR, NPM1 wild-type) in CR1, MRD-negative. This is a Category 1 indication for alloHSCT in CR1 per ELN 2022, NCCN, and published randomized data from the AMLSG and MRC collaborative groups. Without transplant, the relapse risk for this disease biology at 2 years is estimated at 60–70% with chemotherapy alone, even with midostaurin maintenance.
   - DONOR: 10/10 HLA allele-level matched unrelated donor (MUD), 38-year-old male, CMV negative. This is the best available alternative to a matched sibling donor and outcomes with a 10/10 MUD are comparable to matched related donor transplants in this disease setting.
   - STEM CELL SOURCE: Peripheral blood stem cells (PBSC)
   - CONDITIONING REGIMEN PLANNED: Myeloablative conditioning with fludarabine/busulfan (FluBu4) — Fludarabine 40 mg/m2/day x 4 days (days -6 to -3) + Busulfan 3.2 mg/kg/day IV x 4 days (days -6 to -3). PK-guided busulfan dosing to target AUC 4500–6000 micromol*min/L will be employed. Rationale: FluBu4 myeloablative conditioning is selected given patient's young age (48), excellent performance status, normal organ function, and the need for maximum anti-leukemic intensity in this high-risk, aggressive disease. The myeloablative approach provides a more potent graft-versus-leukemia (GVL) effect compared to reduced-intensity conditioning, which is critical for this FLT3-ITD high-risk case.
   - GVHD PROPHYLAXIS: Tacrolimus/Trexall combination. Tacrolimus IV initiated on day -3, targeting a trough level of 8–12 ng/mL. Short-course Trexall 15 mg/m2 IV on day +1, 10 mg/m2 on days +3, +6, and +11. Additionally, rabbit anti-thymocyte globulin (ATG, Thymoglobulin) 2.5 mg/kg/day x 3 days (days -3, -2, -1; total 7.5 mg/kg) will be administered for additional GVHD prophylaxis and to facilitate engraftment in the MUD setting, per institutional protocol.
   - Discussed with patient the risks and benefits of the transplant procedure in detail, including:
     a. Transplant-related mortality (TRM) at 1 year estimated 5–10% for a patient of this profile (young, good PS, MUD 10/10, first CR)
     b. Acute GVHD risk (Grade II–IV: ~25–35% with tacrolimus/MTX/ATG; Grade III–IV: ~10–15%)
     c. Chronic GVHD risk (~40–50% with PBSC source; though most cases are mild-moderate and manageable)
     d. Infectious risks during the immunocompromised period (bacterial, fungal, viral including CMV — noted above; PCP)
     e. SOS/VOD risk with busulfan-based conditioning (use of PK dosing mitigates this risk)
     f. Pulmonary complications including diffuse alveolar hemorrhage, idiopathic pneumonia syndrome
     g. Long-term effects: gonadal failure and premature ovarian insufficiency (highly likely given conditioning; patient already has chemotherapy-induced amenorrhea; reproductive endocrinology referral placed today for counseling and fertility discussion, recognizing that fertility preservation before transplant is not feasible at this timeline given urgency; hormone replacement therapy options post-transplant will be discussed)
     h. Secondary malignancy risk (very low in the short-term but not zero at 10–20 years; skin cancers and post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorder are rare known late risks)
     i. Tacrolimus nephrotoxicity with prolonged use
   - Patient and her husband attended this discussion. Patient confirms understanding and informed consent obtained. Patient wishes to proceed.

2. BRIDGING CONSOLIDATION PRIOR TO TRANSPLANT:
   Given the approximately 4-week interval until the transplant date (required for donor mobilization and travel logistics), we plan to administer one cycle of bridging intermediate-dose cytarabine (IDAC) consolidation to maintain remission and reduce risk of relapse during the pre-transplant window. This is standard practice at our institution for patients awaiting MUD transplant. Specific regimen to be discussed at next visit; cytarabine 1.5 g/m2 IV every 12 hours on days 1, 3, and 5 is planned. Midostaurin will be continued on days 8–21 per protocol. Patient is aware that another hospitalization will be required for this consolidation cycle and has agreed.

3. CMV MANAGEMENT PLAN:
   - Patient is CMV IgG positive (seropositive recipient); donor is CMV IgG negative. This D-/R+ pairing is the highest-risk CMV serostatus combination.
   - Plan: Weekly quantitative CMV PCR from blood starting at approximately day +14 post-transplant, continuing through at least day +100, and longer if on immunosuppression beyond that point.
   - Pre-emptive therapy approach: If CMV viremia is detected at any level, will initiate pre-emptive valganciclovir (or IV ganciclovir if oral absorption is a concern during mucositis). Letermovir prophylaxis will be considered per transplant protocol beginning day +1.

Peripheral blood stem cells were infused on day 0 without adverse reactions; CD34+ cell dose was 6.8 x 10^6/kg.

The post-transplant period (days +7 through +18) was marked by expected but significant early toxicities: grade 3 oral/GI mucositis (managed with IV morphine PCA and total parenteral nutrition for 8 days), grade 2 nausea and vomiting, febrile neutropenia on day +8 (blood cultures negative; treated empirically with IV meropenem with clinical resolution), and mild sinusoidal obstruction syndrome (peak bilirubin 2.8 mg/dL and ALT 142 U/L on day +12, meeting Modified Seattle Criteria for mild SOS; managed conservatively with fluid restriction and weight monitoring, resolving by day +20 without defibrotide). Weekly CMV surveillance by quantitative PCR was initiated at day +14 given the CMV+/- donor-recipient pairing. CMV viremia was first detected at day +28 (460 IU/mL), and pre-emptive therapy with valganciclovir was started. The patient was discharged from the inpatient unit and presents today for her first major outpatient follow-up visit.

She reports markedly improved energy compared to the peak of her mucositis. She is eating and drinking adequately by mouth, having fully resumed an oral diet approximately 10 days ago. She reports mild fatigue with moderate exertion, which she acknowledges is an improvement from her inpatient experience. No fever, chills, or rigors since day +22. She is taking valganciclovir, tacrolimus, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, acyclovir, and fluconazole by mouth without difficulty. She reports no rash, no skin changes, no yellowing of eyes or skin, and no leg swelling. Appetite is approximately 75% of baseline. She denies any new bleeding, bruising, headache, or visual changes.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Ms. Kowalczyk is a 48-year-old woman with de novo AML, FAB M4, FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio (ELN Adverse risk), who achieved complete morphologic and MRD-negative remission after 7+3 plus midostaurin induction and subsequently underwent myeloablative alloHSCT from a 10/10 MUD with FluBu4 conditioning and tacrolimus/Trexall/ATG GVHD prophylaxis. Today's day +30 assessment is overall very encouraging.

1. ENGRAFTMENT - FULL DONOR CHIMERISM ACHIEVED:
Excellent engraftment demonstrated. Neutrophil engraftment occurred on day +14 and platelet engraftment on day +22, both within expected windows for a PBSC graft with this conditioning platform. Today's CBC shows a WBC of 4,200 with an ANC of 3,100 and platelets of 112,000, reflecting robust early count recovery. Peripheral blood chimerism at day +28 confirms 99% donor engraftment by STR analysis, with 100% donor in the myeloid fraction - this is essentially complete donor engraftment and is highly reassuring. No further chimerism testing is required at this early timepoint beyond confirming today's result.
 - Plan: Repeat peripheral blood chimerism at approximately day +90 and day +150. Bone marrow biopsy with MRD assessment and cytogenetics planned at approximately day +60 per protocol.

2. DISEASE STATUS - COMPLETE REMISSION, MAINTAINED:
The patient remains in complete remission by CBC morphology (no circulating blasts) and clinical assessment. She has no constitutional symptoms to suggest relapse (no fever, night sweats, new bone pain, or unexplained cytopenias). No lymphadenopathy or organomegaly on exam. Given the high-risk molecular profile (FLT3-ITD, ELN Adverse) and the well-recognized early relapse risk in this subgroup, vigilance is paramount. The outstanding MRD status at the time of transplant and the rapidly established full donor chimerism are both favorable prognostic indicators at this early post-transplant juncture.
 - Plan: Bone marrow biopsy with multiparameter flow cytometry MRD (sensitivity 0.01%), FLT3-ITD PCR, DNMT3A R882H ddPCR, and cytogenetics (to confirm XY donor karyotype) at day +60. Any new unexplained cytopenias or falling chimerism will prompt urgent marrow evaluation.

3. CMV VIREMIA - PRE-EMPTIVE TREATMENT INITIATED, RESPONDING:
CMV viremia was detected at day +28 (460 IU/mL) in the setting of this CMV+/- (recipient positive/donor negative) pairing, which carries a well-established higher risk of CMV reactivation. Pre-emptive valganciclovir 900 mg twice daily was appropriately started. Today's repeat CMV PCR drawn at day +35 returns at 210 IU/mL - a reassuring decline by more than 50% over one week, suggesting good virologic response to valganciclovir. The patient reports no CMV end-organ symptoms (no visual changes, no diarrhea, no dyspnea).
 - Plan: Continue valganciclovir 900 mg PO BID. Recheck CMV PCR weekly. Target two consecutive undetectable results before considering discontinuation. Monitor CBC weekly given valganciclovir myelosuppressive potential; adjust dose if cytopenias develop. Counsel patient to report any visual symptoms, increased diarrhea, or respiratory changes.
 - Note: Tacrolimus-related immunosuppression and the CMV-naive donor graft are the primary drivers of reactivation risk in this patient. The risk should diminish as tacrolimus is tapered and as donor-derived CMV-specific T-cell immunity reconstitutes over the coming months.

4. ACUTE GVHD - NONE IDENTIFIED:
The patient has no current signs or symptoms of acute GVHD. Skin: no rash. Liver: bilirubin and transaminases have normalized (prior mild bilirubin elevation on day +12 was attributed to mild SOS, now fully resolved). GI: no diarrhea, no nausea requiring intervention. The ATG incorporated into her conditioning, combined with tacrolimus/Trexall prophylaxis, appears to have been effective. This is a favorable finding at day +30. Risk for acute GVHD extends through day +100, and the patient and her husband have been counseled to report any new rash, jaundice, diarrhea, or abdominal cramping promptly.
 - Plan: Tacrolimus continued at current dose. Trough target remains 8-12 ng/mL through at least day +90, then reassess for taper initiation based on GVHD status and chimerism. Educate patient on signs of acute and chronic GVHD.

5. SINUSOIDAL OBSTRUCTION SYNDROME (SOS/VOD) - RESOLVED:
Mild SOS (bilirubin peak 2.8 mg/dL, ALT 142 U/L on day +12, meeting Modified Seattle Criteria for mild disease) managed conservatively with fluid restriction and weight monitoring during inpatient stay. Bilirubin today is 0.9 mg/dL and ALT 38 U/L - complete biochemical resolution. No defibrotide was required. No further SOS monitoring needed at this time.

6. ELECTROLYTES AND RENAL FUNCTION - TACROLIMUS-RELATED ISSUES:
Known tacrolimus-related hypomagnesemia (magnesium 1.7 mEq/L, low-normal) and mild hypokalemia (potassium 3.6 mEq/L) are being managed with ongoing oral replacement. Creatinine 0.94 mg/dL with eGFR ~74 mL/min/1.73m2 represents improvement from inpatient peak creatinine (1.18 mg/dL), likely reflecting recovery from ATG administration and early tacrolimus effects. Renal function will remain under close surveillance; if eGFR deteriorates below 60 mL/min, tacrolimus dose adjustment and nephrology consultation will be considered.
 - Plan: Continue electrolyte supplementation. Monitor BMP, tacrolimus trough, and creatinine weekly.

7. TYPE 2 DIABETES / GLYCEMIC MANAGEMENT:
Metformin 500 mg BID carefully restarted after confirming adequate renal function (eGFR >60). HbA1c today at 7.4% is mildly elevated from her baseline of 7.2%, likely reflecting tacrolimus-induced insulin resistance and the metabolic stress of transplant. Fasting glucose of 118 mg/dL is acceptable. No symptomatic hyperglycemia.
 - Plan: Continue metformin 500 mg BID; up-titration can be considered at next visit if tolerated and renal function remains stable. If persistent hyperglycemia on tacrolimus, may need to involve endocrinology or add a second agent. Home blood glucose monitoring encouraged.

8. NUTRITIONAL RECOVERY AND WEIGHT:
Weight today is 62.5 kg, down approximately 6.7% from her pre-illness weight of 67 kg but significantly improved from her nadir weight during inpatient admission. She has fully resumed oral diet. Appetite at 75% of baseline is acceptable at day +30. Oral mucositis has resolved. She is no longer on TPN.
 - Plan: Encourage high-protein, high-calorie diet. Dietary counseling referral provided. If weight does not recover over next 4-6 weeks, reassess with nutrition services.

9. INFECTIOUS PROPHYLAXIS:
Current prophylaxis regimen: TMP-SMX (PCP prophylaxis), acyclovir (HSV/VZV prophylaxis), fluconazole (antifungal prophylaxis). This will be maintained while patient is on tacrolimus immunosuppression. Valganciclovir is serving dual purpose of CMV pre-emptive treatment and additional herpesvirus coverage. No antifungal escalation needed at this time (no radiographic changes suggestive of mold infection, no fever).

10. PERFORMANCE STATUS AND FUNCTIONAL RECOVERY:
ECOG PS 1 today - patient is ambulatory, managing her ADLs independently at home, and reports improved energy trajectory. She is not yet back to work (currently on medical leave, appropriate). She is living at home with husband support, which is optimal for post-transplant monitoring and adherence.
 - Plan: Physical therapy and occupational therapy referral to support deconditioning recovery. Discuss return-to-work timeline at future visits (target approximately month 4-5 if recovery continues on current trajectory).

11. MIDOSTAURIN POST-TRANSPLANT:
Midostaurin (FLT3 inhibitor, PKC412) was used as part of induction and bridging consolidation per RATIFY trial standard of care. Post-transplant FLT3 inhibitor maintenance (e.g., gilteritinib or sora maintenance post-alloHSCT) is not currently part of our institutional protocol at this time given the pre-existing clinical data landscape; this will be reassessed as evidence matures. Patient is not currently on midostaurin or any targeted therapy post-transplant.
 - Plan: Continue to monitor emerging data on post-transplant FLT3 inhibitor maintenance strategies. Raise at tumor board if MRD status or chimerism raises any concern at day +60.

12. CARE COORDINATION AND FOLLOW-UP:
Patient has been given detailed written and verbal instructions on signs and symptoms requiring urgent evaluation (fever >38.0°C, new rash, jaundice, significant diarrhea, bleeding, dyspnea, visual changes). Emergency contact numbers provided. She understands to present to the emergency department or call the transplant clinic immediately for any concerning symptoms.

Day +60 post-transplant: Bone marrow biopsy (MRD by MFC and FLT3-ITD PCR, cytogenetics, DNMT3A R882H ddPCR, chimerism on sorted CD34+ progenitors). Peripheral blood chimerism. Full labs. Clinic assessment.

COMMENT
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
This day +60 post-transplant bone marrow evaluation demonstrates sustained complete morphologic remission with no evidence of residual or recurrent acute myeloid leukemia. Hematopoiesis is trilineage and orderly, with cellularity appropriate for the post-transplant interval. The blast count is <2% by both morphology and flow cytometry immunophenotyping. MRD assessment by multiparameter flow cytometry is negative at high sensitivity (0.01%). The FLT3-ITD mutation, which served as the index molecular marker in this patient's AML, is undetectable by PCR (sensitivity 10^-4).

The cytogenetic finding of an exclusively male karyotype (46,XY) in this female patient provides definitive cytogenetic confirmation of complete donor engraftment, which is further substantiated by STR chimerism demonstrating 100% donor in the CD34+ progenitor compartment.

The low-level DNMT3A R882H signal (VAF 2.1%) detected by ddPCR is interpreted as consistent with residual host non-hematopoietic or stromal elements harboring a pre-existing CHIP clone, and not as evidence of AML persistence or recurrence. This interpretation is supported by: (1) undetectable FLT3-ITD (the driver mutation of the AML clone); (2) 100% donor chimerism in CD34+ progenitors; (3) absence of any morphologic or immunophenotypic evidence of AML; and (4) stability of the signal compared to prior assessment. Serial monitoring of this low-level signal is recommended.

Overall, these findings are highly favorable and consistent with deep molecular remission and complete donor engraftment at day +60 following myeloablative allogeneic HSCT for FLT3-ITD-mutant AML.

FISH panel results will be addended when available from the reference laboratory.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Kowalczyk is a 49-year-old female with a history of de novo AML (FAB M4, FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, ELN Adverse risk) who was diagnosed approximately 3.5 months ago and has now reached the 100-day post-transplant milestone. For full background: she initially presented with a 3-week history of fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, spontaneous gingival bleeding, easy bruising, and night sweats. Labs at that time demonstrated a white count of 87,400/microliter, hemoglobin 7.2 g/dL, and platelets of 34,000/microliter. Bone marrow biopsy confirmed 74% blasts consistent with AML (FAB M4), with FLT3-ITD at high allelic ratio (VAF 82%), DNMT3A R882H co-mutation, and normal 46,XX karyotype. She was classified as ELN 2022 Adverse risk given the FLT3-ITD at high allelic ratio in the setting of wild-type NPM1.

She received standard 7+3 induction chemotherapy with cytarabine (200 mg/m2/day continuous infusion, days 1–7) and daunorubicin (60 mg/m2 on days 1–3) plus midostaurin (50 mg BID on days 8–21). Her induction course was complicated by febrile neutropenia with E. coli bacteremia (pan-sensitive, treated with ceftriaxone), grade 3 mucositis, and a prolonged neutropenic period of 22 days. Day 28 bone marrow showed complete morphologic remission with MRD negativity by multiparameter flow cytometry and undetectable FLT3-ITD by PCR. Given her Adverse risk classification, she was counseled on and agreed to proceed with allogeneic stem cell transplant in first CR.

HLA typing identified no matched sibling donor (1/2 siblings was a 7/10 mismatch). A 10/10 allele-level matched unrelated donor (MUD) was identified through NMDP — a 38-year-old CMV-negative male. She underwent one cycle of bridging consolidation with intermediate-dose cytarabine (IDAC, 1.5 g/m2 q12h on days 1, 3, 5) to maintain remission during the donor workup period, complicated by grade 2 bilateral cytarabine-related conjunctivitis (managed with prophylactic steroid eye drops per protocol), which fully resolved. Her post-consolidation assessment confirmed ongoing MRD-negative CR.

She was admitted approximately 14 weeks after initial diagnosis for myeloablative conditioning with fludarabine (40 mg/m2/day × 4 days) and busulfan (3.2 mg/kg/day × 4 days, FluBu4) with PK-guided busulfan dosing to achieve target AUC. GVHD prophylaxis consisted of tacrolimus/methotrexate with rabbit ATG (thymoglobulin 2.5 mg/kg/day × 3 days). Peripheral blood stem cells were infused on day 0 (CD34+ cell dose 6.8 × 10^6/kg) without infusion reactions.

Post-transplant course was notable for:
- Grade 3 mucositis (day +7 to +18), exacerbated by methotrexate; managed with IV morphine PCA, TPN for approximately 8 days
- Febrile neutropenia at day +8 (blood cultures negative); treated empirically with IV meropenem
- Mild sinusoidal obstruction syndrome (SOS/VOD) meeting Modified Seattle Criteria — bilirubin peaked at 2.8 mg/dL on day +12, ALT 142 U/L; managed conservatively with fluid restriction and daily weights; fully resolved without defibrotide by day +20

- Neutrophil engraftment achieved on day +14 (ANC >500/microliter × 3 days)

At her day +30 visit, she had achieved full count recovery (WBC 4,200, ANC 3,100, Hgb 10.2, platelets 112,000). Chimerism at day +28 confirmed 99% donor chimerism by STR analysis. ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Kowalczyk is a 49-year-old woman who has reached day +100 following myeloablative allogeneic HSCT (10/10 MUD, FluBu4, PBSC) for high-risk AML (FLT3-ITD high AR, ELN Adverse). She is in sustained complete morphologic and molecular remission, with near-normalized blood counts, complete donor chimerism, and no evidence of AML recurrence. This is an excellent response. The major new finding today is early chronic GVHD, which I will discuss below.

1. AML — Sustained Complete Remission, MRD Negative
Her most recent bone marrow at day +60 showed MRD negativity by high-sensitivity flow cytometry (sensitivity 0.01%) with undetectable FLT3-ITD by PCR and 100% donor chimerism on sorted CD34+ progenitors. The peripheral blood chimerism at day +90 confirms complete donor engraftment (100% donor). CBC today shows near-normal counts (ANC 3,900, platelets 148,000, Hgb 11.4 g/dL). CT imaging at day +90 is reassuring with no extramedullary disease. She has no symptoms suggestive of relapse (no B symptoms, no new cytopenias, no bone pain). Overall, her disease status is excellent at the 100-day milestone.
- Continue observation for relapse. Next surveillance bone marrow biopsy planned at day +180 (approximately 6 months post-transplant)
- Continue monthly peripheral blood chimerism monitoring
- Patient and family reminded to report any new fevers, unexpected fatigue, bruising, or bleeding

2. Chronic GVHD — New Diagnosis, Mild (NIH Consensus Criteria)
The patient's new symptoms of bilateral dry eyes, xerostomia, and the newly identified lichenoid skin changes on bilateral forearms are consistent with early chronic GVHD. This is a recognized complication following allogeneic transplantation, particularly with MUD donors, and typically manifests after day +100. The timing and organ involvement are classic.

NIH Consensus Global Severity Staging (2014 criteria):
- Skin: Score 1 (involvement of <18% BSA with lichenoid features; estimated ~7–8% BSA forearms bilaterally; no sclerotic features, bullae, or ulceration)
- Eyes: Score 1 (symptomatic dry eyes with Schirmer's test below normal, not severely impacting daily activities; no corneal ulceration)
- Mouth: Score 1 (xerostomia, mild sensitivity; no frank ulceration or substantial limitation in oral intake)
- No involvement of lungs, liver, GI tract, esophagus, genitalia, or joints/fascia
- Overall NIH Global Severity: Mild chronic GVHD (≥2 organs involved, all mild, no moderate or severe organ scores)

Given NIH mild global severity, systemic corticosteroids are not indicated at this time. Plan for topical/supportive management:

  a) Skin: Topical tacrolimus 0.1% ointment (Protopic) to affected forearm areas twice daily; for more symptomatic areas of induration, add clobetasol 0.05% ointment sparingly under occlusion; counsel on sun avoidance, daily sunscreen use, and moisturizer application; educate patient to report any new skin changes, tightening elsewhere, or bullous/ulcerative changes
  b) Eyes: Preservative-free artificial tears (e.g., Refresh or equivalent) every 1–2 hours as needed; lubricating ophthalmic gel at bedtime; refer to ophthalmology for cyclosporine 0.05% ophthalmic emulsion (Restasis) consideration and further dry eye evaluation; remind patient to wear UV-protective sunglasses outdoors
  c) Oral: Biotene toothpaste and oral rinse for xerostomia; frequent sips of water; avoid alcohol-containing mouthwashes; referral to dental care for caries risk counseling (increased risk in cGVHD with sicca); salivary stimulants (sugar-free gum, malic acid lozenges) may help
  d) Systemic immunosuppression: Continue tacrolimus per current taper schedule — trough now 7.4 ng/mL; given the development of mild cGVHD, we will proceed cautiously with the taper but will not accelerate it. New target trough 5–8 ng/mL. Plan to taper to 5 ng/mL trough over the next 4–6 weeks if no progression
  e) Referrals: Ophthalmology (dry eye/cGVHD evaluation); dermatology (cGVHD skin evaluation and biopsy if needed for confirmation)
  f) Monitoring: Document any new organ involvement, increase in BSA involvement, or worsening of current sites at each visit; institute formal NIH cGVHD scoring at each visit going forward

3. CMV Reactivation — Resolved
CMV viremia (first detected day +28 at 460 IU/mL) was successfully treated with valganciclovir and cleared with two consecutive undetectable PCRs. Valganciclovir was discontinued at day +84. CMV PCR today is undetectable. Continue weekly CMV surveillance per transplant protocol for now; can transition to biweekly surveillance by day +120 given now-stable status and ongoing tacrolimus-based immunosuppression.

4. Peripheral Neuropathy (Cytarabine-Related, Grade 1)
Bilateral hand tingling, attributed to cytarabine from the IDAC consolidation cycle, remains stable and mildly improving. No motor deficits. No new neurologic concerns. Continue to monitor; no intervention needed at this time. Consider neurology referral only if symptoms worsen.

5. Tacrolimus / Immunosuppression Management
Tacrolimus trough today 7.4 ng/mL, within target range. Per protocol, slow taper initiated today with target trough 5–8 ng/mL over the next 4–6 weeks, with a long-term plan for tapering off by month 18 post-transplant if cGVHD remains stable and mild. Given today's identification of mild cGVHD, we will proceed with taper but not accelerate.

- Continue TMP-SMX (Bactrim) for PCP prophylaxis while on immunosuppression
- Continue acyclovir for HSV/VZV prophylaxis while on immunosuppression

6. Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus
Metformin was resumed at day +90 after confirming stable renal function. HbA1c today 7.0%, reflecting adequate glycemic control in the context of ongoing tacrolimus use (calcineurin inhibitor-related insulin resistance is a known contributor). Encourage continued dietary modification; will communicate with primary care physician for ongoing diabetes management co-management. No changes to current regimen today.

7. Functional Status and Psychosocial Assessment
Patient reports improving energy. ECOG PS 1 (mild fatigue but ambulatory and capable of light activity). Weight 63.5 kg (down ~5% from baseline of 67 kg, stable). She is doing some light household activities and is hopeful about returning to her teaching position. Mild anxiety around the risk of relapse is noted; she is using coping strategies and has strong family support. Referred to our transplant survivorship support group. Social work involvement offered and accepted for ongoing psychosocial support.

COMMENT
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The morphologic, immunohistochemical, flow cytometric, cytochemical, and molecular findings are integrated and diagnostic of de novo acute myeloid leukemia (AML) with monocytic differentiation, FAB M4 (acute myelomonocytic leukemia), WHO 2022 category "AML, NOS" with monocytic differentiation, with a concurrent FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio mutation and DNMT3A R882H co-mutation.

The blast count of 74% (by WHO 2022 criteria, combining myeloblasts and promonocytes as blast equivalents) substantially exceeds the 20% threshold required for diagnosis of AML, and is consistent with the presenting peripheral blood findings of marked leukocytosis (WBC 87,400/µL) with circulating blasts.

The identification of rare Auer rods confirms myeloid lineage and is consistent with FAB M4 morphology; notably, Auer rods can be seen in both myeloblasts and occasionally in abnormal promyelocytes in AML but were not observed in the monocytic component in this case.

The combined myeloperoxidase positivity (myeloid component) and non-specific esterase positivity (monocytic component) by cytochemistry, confirmed by immunophenotyping (CD64 bright, CD14 partial), is the hallmark of acute myelomonocytic leukemia (FAB M4). This is in contrast to FAB M5 (pure monocytic/monoblastic AML), in which the monocytic component would predominate (>80% blasts of monocytic lineage).

The normal karyotype (46,XX) indicates this is not a core-binding factor AML (no t(8;21) or inv(16)) and not acute promyelocytic leukemia (no t(15;17)), which are distinguished by their specific morphologic, immunophenotypic, and molecular features. In the absence of AML-defining chromosomal abnormalities and in the context of the identified FLT3-ITD mutation with high allelic ratio (>0.5) without NPM1 co-mutation, this case is classified as ELN 2022 Adverse risk.

There is no morphologic or cytogenetic evidence of a pre-existing myelodysplastic syndrome or therapy-related process. The diagnosis is de novo AML.

The DNMT3A R882H mutation (VAF ~47%), detected at approximately 50% variant allele frequency, is consistent with a founding clonal event and is commonly co-mutated with FLT3-ITD in de novo AML. DNMT3A mutations at R882 are known to impair DNMT3A tetramerization and reduce enzymatic activity, resulting in focal DNA hypomethylation. While also a recognized clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential (CHIP) driver gene, in the current context (74% blasts, high VAF), this mutation is considered part of the leukemic clone rather than pre-existing CHIP.

The LAIP (leukemia-associated immunophenotype) established by flow cytometry — CD45dim/CD34+/CD117+/HLA-DR+/CD64bright/CD33bright/MPO+ — will serve as the reference immunophenotype for subsequent minimal residual disease (MRD) monitoring by multiparameter flow cytometry following therapy.

This day +365 bone marrow evaluation demonstrates sustained complete morphologic and molecular remission in a patient with previously high-risk AML (FLT3-ITD, high allelic ratio; ELN Adverse) following myeloablative allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation from a 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated donor. Complete donor chimerism is confirmed by both conventional cytogenetics (46,XY replacing the patient's prior 46,XX karyotype) and STR analysis (100% donor in peripheral blood and CD34+ progenitor fractions). The disappearance of the previously detected low-level DNMT3A R882H signal (VAF ~2% at day +60, attributed to pre-existing CHIP within residual host hematopoiesis) is consistent with complete replacement of host hematopoiesis by the donor graft. FLT3-ITD remains undetectable at the 10^-4 sensitivity threshold of the fragment analysis assay. The overall findings at one year post-transplant are highly favorable and consistent with durable disease control. The small non-paratrabecular lymphoid aggregate and mildly increased hematogones are expected post-transplant immune reconstitution findings and do not suggest PTLD or lymphoma; clinical correlation is recommended if interval changes occur.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Renata Kowalczyk is a 50-year-old female with a history of de novo acute myeloid leukemia (AML), FAB M4 subtype (acute myelomonocytic leukemia), diagnosed approximately 18 months prior at age 48. Her AML was molecularly characterized by a FLT3-ITD mutation carrying a high allelic ratio (VAF 0.82, >48 bp insertion), placing her in the ELN 2022 Adverse risk category. She had a co-occurring DNMT3A R882H mutation, felt to represent a pre-existing clonal hematopoiesis (CHIP) clone. NPM1, IDH1/2, and CEBPA were all wild-type. Cytogenetics showed a normal 46,XX karyotype.

She achieved a complete morphologic remission (CR) with MRD negativity (both by multiparameter flow cytometry and FLT3-ITD PCR) after a single cycle of 7+3 induction chemotherapy with cytarabine and daunorubicin, augmented by midostaurin (Rydapt) as per the RATIFY trial protocol. Given her adverse-risk disease, she was referred expeditiously for allogeneic stem cell transplantation in first CR. No fully HLA-matched sibling was available; a 10/10 allele-level matched unrelated donor (MUD) - a 38-year-old CMV-seronegative male - was identified through the NMDP. She received one cycle of bridging consolidation with intermediate-dose cytarabine (IDAC) to maintain remission during the donor workup interval.

She underwent myeloablative conditioning with fludarabine/busulfan (FluBu4) with PK-guided busulfan dosing, GVHD prophylaxis with tacrolimus/short-course MTX, and anti-thymocyte globulin (thymoglobulin) for the MUD setting. Peripheral blood stem cell infusion (CD34+ dose 6.8 x 10^6/kg) was uneventful. Early post-transplant course was notable for grade 3 mucositis, febrile neutropenia with bacteremia (E. coli during induction, negative cultures peri-transplant), mild sinusoidal obstruction syndrome (SOS) meeting Modified Seattle Criteria - resolved without defibrotide - and CMV viremia detected at day +28 (460 IU/mL) treated with valganciclovir with subsequent clearance. Neutrophil engraftment occurred at day +14, platelet engraftment at day +22.

She developed mild chronic GVHD at approximately day +100, involving skin (lichenoid plaques over forearms and shins, BSA <10%), eyes (dry eye syndrome, Schirmer test 7 mm/5 min), and oral mucosa (xerostomia). NIH global severity was rated as Mild. This was managed exclusively with topical therapies (topical tacrolimus 0.1% ointment, clobetasol 0.05% ointment for skin; cyclosporine 0.05% ophthalmic emulsion and preservative-free artificial tears for eyes; Biotene products for oral dryness), along with continuation of tacrolimus as systemic immunosuppression. She did not require systemic corticosteroids at any point.

Disease surveillance has been reassuring at every timepoint. Bone marrow biopsies at day +60 and day +365 both confirmed sustained complete morphologic and molecular remission with MRD negativity. Peripheral blood chimerism has been 100% donor since day +150. The 46,XY karyotype on marrow specimens confirms complete donor engraftment from the male donor. DNMT3A R882H ddPCR signal was undetectable on the day +365 biopsy, suggesting even the CHIP clone has been replaced by donor hematopoiesis.

At today's visit (approximately day +540, month 18 post-transplant), she presents for her scheduled milestone visit, which marks the planned endpoint of tacrolimus taper and transition off all systemic immunosuppression. She reports feeling well overall. She returned to her position as a high school biology teacher part-time at month 4 and resumed full-time work at month 5 post-transplant, which she continues. She denies fatigue beyond normal baseline, reports no new skin changes, no increased dryness in eyes or mouth, no dyspnea, no fevers, no night sweats, and no bleeding. Ms. Kowalczyk is a 50-year-old female presenting for her 18-month post-allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant (alloHSCT) survivorship follow-up. She feels well overall and has no acute complaints. HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Kowalczyk was diagnosed approximately 18 months ago with de novo acute myeloid leukemia, FAB M4 (acute myelomonocytic leukemia), presenting with marked leukocytosis (WBC 87,400/microliter), symptomatic anemia (hemoglobin 7.2 g/dL), and thrombocytopenia (platelets 34,000/microliter). The diagnosis was confirmed by bone marrow biopsy revealing 74% blasts with monocytic differentiation, Auer rods on peripheral smear, and immunophenotype consistent with AML (CD34+, CD117+, CD33+, CD13+, MPO+, CD64+). Molecular profiling by NGS identified a high allelic ratio FLT3-ITD mutation (VAF 0.82, allelic ratio >0.5) and a co-occurring DNMT3A R882H mutation (VAF 47%). NPM1, IDH1/IDH2, CEBPA, and all other actionable AML mutations were wild-type. Cytogenetics demonstrated a normal karyotype (46,XX). Per ELN 2022 risk stratification, she was classified as Adverse risk, primarily driven by the high allelic ratio FLT3-ITD in the absence of NPM1 mutation.

She received induction chemotherapy with the 7+3 regimen (cytarabine 200 mg/m2/day continuous infusion days 1-7, daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 days 1-3) plus midostaurin 50 mg twice daily (days 8-21) per the RATIFY protocol. Induction was complicated by febrile neutropenia with E. coli bacteremia (treated with IV ceftriaxone following initial piperacillin-tazobactam empirics), Grade 3 mucositis, and a 22-day period of neutropenia requiring 4 units of packed red blood cells and 8 pooled platelet transfusions. Despite this challenging course, day 28 bone marrow assessment demonstrated complete morphologic remission with MRD negativity by multiparameter flow cytometry (sensitivity 0.01%) and FLT3-ITD PCR. Cytogenetics normalized to 46,XX.

Given the Adverse ELN risk classification, the decision was made to pursue allogeneic transplant in first CR. HLA typing revealed no matched sibling donor. An unrelated donor search through NMDP/Be The Match rapidly identified a 10/10 allele-level matched unrelated donor (38-year-old CMV-negative male). Pre-transplant evaluation confirmed adequate end-organ function (LVEF 62%, FEV1 88%, DLCO 82% predicted, eGFR 72 mL/min/1.73m2). One cycle of bridging consolidation with intermediate-dose cytarabine (IDAC, 1.5 g/m2 q12h days 1, 3, 5) plus midostaurin was administered during the donor workup period and was complicated by Grade 2 cytarabine-associated bilateral conjunctivitis (managed with steroid eye drops) and Grade 1 peripheral neuropathy.

She proceeded to myeloablative conditioning with fludarabine 40 mg/m2/day x 4 days and busulfan 3.2 mg/kg/day x 4 days (FluBu4) with PK-directed dose adjustment to target AUC. GVHD prophylaxis consisted of tacrolimus, short-course MTX (day +1, +3, +6, +11), and rabbit ATG (thymoglobulin, total 7.5 mg/kg). Peripheral blood stem cells were infused on day 0 (CD34+ dose 6.8 x 10^6/kg). The early post-transplant course was complicated by Grade 3 mucositis (requiring morphine PCA and TPN for 8 days), CMV reactivation on day +28 (treated with valganciclovir, resolved by day +70-84), and mild sinusoidal obstruction syndrome (bilirubin peak 2.8 mg/dL, resolved without defibrotide). Neutrophil engraftment occurred on day +14 and platelet engraftment on day +22. Day +28 chimerism was 99% donor by STR analysis.

Day +60 bone marrow biopsy confirmed sustained CR with MRD negativity, and karyotype confirmed 46,XY (complete male donor engraftment). At day +100, she developed mild chronic GVHD affecting skin (lichenoid plaques on forearms and shins, BSA <10%), eyes (dry eye syndrome), and oral mucosa (xerostomia), meeting NIH global severity criteria for mild cGVHD. This was managed with topical tacrolimus 0.1% ointment, clobetasol 0.05% ointment, cyclosporine 0.05% ophthalmic emulsion, preservative-free artificial tears, and biotene products. Systemic corticosteroids were not required. The 12-month post-transplant bone marrow biopsy (day +365) confirmed continued MRD-negative complete remission with 100% donor chimerism; the DNMT3A R882H signal was no longer detectable by ddPCR at this timepoint.

At her one-year milestone visit, the following late effect findings were noted: borderline low bone density (Z-score -1.1, started calcium and vitamin D supplementation); early posterior subcapsular cataract in the left eye (attributed to steroid exposure, under ophthalmologic surveillance); premature ovarian insufficiency (FSH 87 mIU/mL, AMH undetectable, referred to reproductive endocrinology); mild tacrolimus-associated nephrotoxicity (eGFR 64 mL/min/1.73m2, stable); and mild insulin resistance/suboptimal diabetes control (HbA1c 7.0% on metformin, attributed in part to tacrolimus). She was scheduled for progressive tacrolimus taper targeting discontinuation today at month 18, contingent on stable cGVHD.

Today's visit: She reports feeling excellent. She has been back to full-time teaching for approximately 13 months. She denies any fever, night sweats, new bruising, or bleeding. Her skin, eyes, and mouth symptoms have continued to improve and she reports only minimal residual dryness (eyes and mouth). She has been adherent with hormone replacement therapy (transdermal estradiol/oral progesterone) and reports significant improvement in hot flashes and energy levels. No new symptoms or concerns.
