SECTION 2: CLINICALLY ACTIONABLE / REPORTABLE VARIANTS

------------------------------------------------------------
GENE:        FLT3
Variant:     Internal Tandem Duplication (ITD)
Location:    Exon 14-15 juxtamembrane domain (JMD)
Insertion Length: 48 base pairs
Allelic Ratio: 0.82 (high; threshold for high AR: >=0.5 per ELN 2022)
VAF:         82%
Method:      Fragment length analysis (capillary electrophoresis) and confirmatory NGS read-pair analysis
Zygosity:    Cannot exclude homozygosity vs. high-AR heterozygosity; allelic ratio >0.5 supports high clonal burden
Classification: Pathogenic / Clinically Actionable
Clinical Significance:
  FLT3-ITD is the most common somatic mutation in AML, occurring in approximately 20-25% of de novo AML cases. Insertions within the juxtamembrane domain lead to constitutive autophosphorylation and activation of downstream pro-survival signaling pathways (PI3K/AKT, RAS/MAPK, STAT5). FLT3-ITD is associated with leukocytosis, high blast burden, and inferior outcomes with standard chemotherapy alone. The allelic ratio (AR) reflects the proportion of FLT3-ITD to wild-type FLT3 alleles; high AR (>=0.5) confers a particularly adverse prognosis and is an independent predictor of relapse and reduced overall survival. This patient's AR of 0.82 places her in the highest-risk FLT3-ITD subgroup.
  Per ELN 2022 guidelines, FLT3-ITD with high AR in the absence of co-occurring NPM1 mutation is classified as ELN Adverse risk, strongly supporting consideration of allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (alloHSCT) in first complete remission (CR1).
  FLT3 inhibitors (midostaurin, gilteritinib, quizartinib) are approved or have demonstrated efficacy in FLT3-mutant AML. The RATIFY trial established midostaurin (50 mg BID, days 8-21) in combination with standard 7+3 induction as standard of care in newly diagnosed FLT3-mutant AML patients aged <=60 years, with a significant improvement in overall survival (HR 0.78, p=0.009). Gilteritinib is approved in the relapsed/refractory setting (ADMIRAL trial).
  Note: FLT3 point mutation (D835/I836, TKD) was not detected (see Section 3). Concurrent FLT3-TKD mutation is not present.
------------------------------------------------------------

------------------------------------------------------------
GENE:        DNMT3A
Variant:     p.R882H (c.2645G>A)
Exon:        Exon 23
VAF:         47%
Depth:       1,183x (563 alt / 1,183 total reads)
Zygosity:    Heterozygous
Classification: Pathogenic
Clinical Significance:
  DNMT3A encodes DNA methyltransferase 3A, an enzyme essential for de novo DNA methylation. The p.R882H hotspot substitution is the most common DNMT3A mutation in AML, accounting for approximately 60-70% of all DNMT3A mutations in myeloid malignancies. The R882 residue is critical for the catalytic activity and oligomerization of DNMT3A; the p.R882H variant acts in a dominant-negative fashion, reducing global DNMT3A methyltransferase activity by approximately 80%.
  DNMT3A R882 mutations occur in approximately 18-22% of de novo AML and are frequently observed as co-mutations with FLT3-ITD (the so-called FLT3-ITD/DNMT3A/NPM1 "triple" and FLT3-ITD/DNMT3A "double" mutant AML subsets). In the absence of NPM1 co-mutation (as in this case), FLT3-ITD/DNMT3A double-mutant AML retains its ELN Adverse classification and is associated with inferior survival outcomes.
  DNMT3A R882H is also a well-characterized clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential (CHIP) mutation and may persist at low VAF after successful AML therapy, representing residual non-malignant CHIP rather than residual disease. The VAF of 47% at diagnosis is consistent with a co-driver role in the leukemic clone (approximately diploid locus, one allele mutated) and contributes to the founding clonal architecture.
  No currently approved therapies specifically target DNMT3A R882H in AML; however, hypomethylating agents (azacitidine, decitabine) have mechanistic rationale and clinical activity in DNMT3A-mutant AML, particularly in the maintenance or salvage setting.

SECTION 8: INTERPRETATION AND MOLECULAR SUMMARY

This comprehensive 523-gene NGS panel performed on bone marrow aspirate from a 48-year-old female with de novo AML (FAB M4, acute myelomonocytic leukemia) identifies two somatic driver mutations:

1. FLT3-ITD (exon 14-15, 48 bp insertion, allelic ratio 0.82): This is the principal oncogenic driver and primary therapeutic target in this patient. The high allelic ratio indicates either acquisition of a second FLT3-ITD allele, loss of heterozygosity at the FLT3 locus with retention of the mutant allele, or a very high clonal frequency of FLT3-ITD-bearing leukemic cells. FLT3-ITD with high AR (>=0.5) in the absence of NPM1 mutation confers ELN 2022 Adverse risk, which is associated with a 4-year overall survival of approximately 25-35% with chemotherapy alone (historical data, pre-FLT3 inhibitor era) and substantially improved outcomes when an allogeneic stem cell transplant is performed in CR1. The addition of midostaurin to induction chemotherapy (RATIFY regimen) is the standard of care for this mutation.

2. DNMT3A p.R882H (VAF 47%): This co-mutation is a known epigenetic driver in AML, co-occurring with FLT3-ITD in approximately 10-15% of cases. As a dominant-negative inhibitor of DNMT3A methyltransferase activity, it leads to focal loss of DNA methylation and transcriptional dysregulation at hematopoietic regulatory loci. DNMT3A R882H is also a CHIP mutation and may persist at very low VAF following successful induction chemotherapy as a residual CHIP clone independent of AML eradication; this must be distinguished from residual AML at MRD assessment.

Molecular profile in the context of AML classification (WHO 2022 / ELN 2022):
  - AML classification: AML, not otherwise specified (NOS) / AML with FLT3-ITD (per 2022 ICC: AML defined by FLT3-ITD)
  - No NPM1 mutation: absent
  - No core binding factor (CBF) rearrangements: absent - confirms non-CBF AML
  - No TP53 mutation: favorable
  - Normal karyotype: not sufficient to shift risk from Adverse in the setting of FLT3-ITD high AR without NPM1

Overall: Molecularly defined de novo AML with FLT3-ITD (high allelic ratio) and DNMT3A R882H co-mutation, normal karyotype, ELN 2022 Adverse risk. The molecular profile strongly supports the use of a FLT3 inhibitor (midostaurin) in combination with induction chemotherapy and proceeding to allogeneic stem cell transplantation in first complete remission.

SECTION 9: TECHNICAL NOTES AND LIMITATIONS

- DNA extraction from bone marrow aspirate; specimen quality adequate (DNA integrity number >7.0; 260/280 ratio 1.84).
- Library preparation: SureSelect XT HS2 hybrid capture; paired-end sequencing.
- FLT3-ITD detection was performed by capillary electrophoresis fragment analysis (gold standard for ITD detection and AR quantification) in addition to NGS-based detection. Fragment analysis is the validated method for FLT3-ITD allelic ratio determination.
- FLT3-TKD (D835/I836) was assessed by both NGS and confirmatory restriction fragment length polymorphism (RFLP) assay; TKD mutation not detected.
- This assay detects somatic variants at VAF >=2% at standard sequencing depth; low-level variants below this threshold may not be detected.
- Copy number analysis is limited in regions of low complexity or near centromeres/telomeres; conventional karyotyping and FISH provide orthogonal confirmation.
- Germline variants cannot be excluded without a paired normal sample. Variants at low VAF or consistent with germline frequency (VAF ~50% or ~100%) should be interpreted with caution without germline confirmation.
- RNA-seq fusion analysis requires adequate RNA quality; RIN >=6.0 confirmed for this sample.
- Results should be interpreted in conjunction with morphologic, immunophenotypic, cytogenetic, and clinical findings.

COMMENTS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. COMPLETE REMISSION (CR) STATUS: This bone marrow examination demonstrates achievement
   of complete morphologic remission (CR) as defined by ELN 2022 and standard AML
   response criteria: <5% blasts by morphology, absence of Auer rods, no circulating
   blasts (see clinical correlation), and recovery of peripheral blood counts (per
   clinical information). The MRD-negative result by high-sensitivity multiparameter
   flow cytometry confers additional favorable prognostic significance (MRD-negative CR,
   CRi if counts not fully recovered — please correlate with concurrent CBC).

2. FLT3-ITD MRD NEGATIVITY: Molecular clearance of FLT3-ITD by PCR is a favorable
   prognostic finding in the context of high allelic ratio FLT3-ITD AML. In patients
   with high allelic ratio FLT3-ITD and NPM1 wild-type (ELN Adverse risk), achieving
   MRD-negative CR after induction is associated with improved outcomes, particularly
   in the context of planned allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation.

3. DNMT3A R882H RESIDUAL SIGNAL: The low-level ddPCR signal for DNMT3A R882H (VAF 1.8%)
   must be interpreted in clinical context. At diagnosis, this mutation was present at
   VAF 47%, co-occurring with FLT3-ITD (VAF 82%). The marked reduction from 47% to 1.8%
   and the clearance of FLT3-ITD confirms effective treatment of the AML clone.
   The residual low-level DNMT3A R882H at VAF ~2% likely represents a pre-existing
   CHIP clone (DNMT3A R882H is one of the most common CHIP mutations) that may have
   been present prior to AML development and is now unmasked in the recovering marrow.
   This does not represent residual AML and does not change the CR designation.
   Serial monitoring for clonal dynamics, including DNMT3A VAF trending, is
   recommended at future timepoints.

4. HYPOCELLULARITY: The 30-40% cellularity is expected at day 28 post-myeloablative
   induction with anthracycline/cytarabine-based therapy. Early trilineage hematopoietic
   recovery is underway. Full count recovery may require several additional weeks.

5. IRON DEFICIENCY: Absent stainable iron stores reflect the combined effect of
   hemolysis, pre-existing anemia, and packed red blood cell transfusion-related
   dilution of reticuloendothelial iron stores. Iron supplementation may be considered
   if peripheral iron studies confirm deficiency; clinical correlation recommended.

CHIEF COMPLAINT:

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Renata Kowalczyk is a 48-year-old female with de novo acute myeloid leukemia (AML), FAB M4 morphology (acute myelomonocytic leukemia), ELN 2022 Adverse risk, presenting today for her day +30 outpatient transplant assessment. She was discharged from the inpatient BMT unit approximately 10 days ago after an expected but challenging post-transplant course.

Briefly, her AML history is as follows: She initially presented approximately 14 weeks ago with a three-week history of profound fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, spontaneous gingival bleeding, and night sweats. Laboratory evaluation at that time revealed a white blood cell count of 87,400/microliter, hemoglobin of 7.2 g/dL, and platelets of 34,000/microliter. Bone marrow biopsy confirmed AML with 74% blasts, FAB M4 morphology, and Auer rods on peripheral smear. Comprehensive molecular workup demonstrated a FLT3-ITD mutation with a very high allelic ratio of 0.82, DNMT3A R882H co-mutation, and otherwise wild-type NPM1, IDH1/IDH2, CEBPA, and TP53, with a normal karyotype (46,XX).

She received induction chemotherapy with the 7+3 regimen (cytarabine 200 mg/m2 continuous infusion days 1-7, daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 days 1-3) with midostaurin 50 mg twice daily added on days 8-21 per the RATIFY trial protocol. Induction was complicated by febrile neutropenia with E. coli bacteremia, grade 3 mucositis, and a 22-day neutropenia duration, all of which resolved. Day 28 bone marrow biopsy confirmed complete morphologic remission (CR1) with MRD negativity by multiparameter flow cytometry (sensitivity 0.01%) and undetectable FLT3-ITD by PCR. Given her ELN Adverse classification driven by FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio without NPM1 mutation, the decision was made to proceed with allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (alloHSCT) in first CR. She underwent one cycle of bridging consolidation with intermediate-dose cytarabine (IDAC, 1.5 g/m2 q12h on days 1, 3, 5) with midostaurin to maintain remission during donor workup, complicated by expected grade 2 cytarabine-associated bilateral conjunctivitis.

A 10/10 allele-level HLA-matched unrelated donor (MUD) was identified through the NMDP — a 38-year-old CMV-negative male. Transplant workup confirmed adequate cardiac function (LVEF 62%), adequate pulmonary function (FEV1 88% predicted, DLCO 82% predicted), and eGFR of 72 mL/min/1.73m2. She was admitted approximately two weeks later for myeloablative conditioning with fludarabine 40 mg/m2/day IV x4 days and busulfan 3.2 mg/kg/day IV x4 days (FluBu4), with busulfan PK-guided dosing to target AUC. GVHD prophylaxis consisted of tacrolimus (started day -3), short-course methotrexate (15 mg/m2 on day +1, 10 mg/m2 on days +3, +6, +11), and rabbit ATG (thymoglobulin, total 7.5 mg/kg over days -3 to -1). Day 0 peripheral blood stem cell infusion was uneventful, with a CD34+ cell dose of 6.8 x 10^6/kg.

Her post-transplant course was notable for several expected toxicities. She developed grade 3 oral and gastrointestinal mucositis, predominantly from day +7 through day +18, exacerbated by methotrexate; this required IV morphine patient-controlled analgesia and total parenteral nutrition for approximately 8 days. She developed febrile neutropenia at day +8 with blood cultures negative; empirical meropenem was initiated with clinical improvement, and she defervesced within 72 hours. She met Modified Seattle Criteria for mild sinusoidal obstruction syndrome (SOS/veno-occlusive disease), with peak bilirubin of 2.8 mg/dL and ALT of 142 U/L at approximately day +12; this was managed conservatively with fluid restriction, careful weight monitoring, and avoidance of nephrotoxins, and resolved without need for defibrotide by day +20. Neutrophil engraftment was documented on day +14 (ANC exceeding 500/microliter for three consecutive days). Platelet engraftment occurred on day +22 (unsupported platelets exceeding 50,000/microliter). She was discharged at approximately day +20 in stable condition with resolving mucositis, adequate oral intake, and recovering counts.

Starting at day +28, CMV surveillance by quantitative PCR detected low-level CMV viremia at 460 IU/mL. Pre-emptive treatment with oral valganciclovir was initiated at that time. This was expected given the CMV mismatch (recipient CMV IgG+, donor CMV IgG−).

Since discharge, Ms. Kowalczyk reports gradual but steady functional improvement. She remains fatigued but is ambulatory without assistance, able to perform activities of daily living independently, and is eating and drinking adequately. She denies fever (she is monitoring her temperature twice daily at home as instructed). She denies new rash, diarrhea, abdominal cramping, or jaundice. She reports no visual changes. She is tolerating all oral medications including tacrolimus and valganciclovir without nausea. She does note mild persistent fatigue and some generalized muscle weakness, which she attributes to deconditioning from her prolonged hospitalization. She denies bleeding, epistaxis, or unusual bruising. No cough or shortness of breath. No dysuria or hematuria.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Ms. Kowalczyk is a 48-year-old woman with de novo AML (FAB M4, FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, ELN Adverse) who achieved complete remission with MRD negativity after 7+3/midostaurin induction, underwent myeloablative FluBu4 conditioning and peripheral blood alloHSCT from a 10/10 MUD, and presents today at day +30 with the following:

1. ENGRAFTMENT — CONFIRMED, EXCELLENT:
Neutrophil engraftment occurred on day +14, platelet engraftment on day +22, both within expected timeframes for a myeloablative PBSC transplant. Current CBC at day +30 shows WBC 4,200/microliter, ANC 3,100/microliter, hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL, and platelets 112,000/microliter — demonstrating robust trilineage recovery. Peripheral blood chimerism at day +28 shows 99% donor cells, confirming nearly complete donor engraftment. This is a highly satisfactory result. We will continue to monitor chimerism monthly; full (100%) donor chimerism is anticipated on subsequent assessments. Bone marrow biopsy for day +60 restaging will be scheduled.

2. ACUTE GVHD — NONE OBSERVED (Grade 0):
Ms. Kowalczyk has had no clinical evidence of acute GVHD at any point post-transplant. No skin rash, no diarrhea, no elevation in bilirubin attributable to hepatic GVHD. This is reassuring and reflects the effectiveness of our ATG-based MUD GVHD prophylaxis strategy combined with tacrolimus/methotrexate. Tacrolimus will be maintained within target trough range of 8-12 ng/mL for the coming weeks. Patient has been counseled regarding warning signs of acute GVHD — particularly skin rash, diarrhea exceeding 500 mL/day, and jaundice — and instructed to contact the transplant clinic immediately if these develop.

3. CMV REACTIVATION — LOW-LEVEL, ON PRE-EMPTIVE VALGANCICLOVIR:
CMV viremia of 460 IU/mL was detected on day +28, as anticipated given the CMV-mismatched donor/recipient pairing (patient CMV IgG+, donor CMV IgG−). Pre-emptive valganciclovir 900 mg twice daily was initiated promptly. Today's repeat CMV PCR has been sent; result pending but will be followed closely. Goal is to document decline/clearance of viremia. CBC will be monitored for valganciclovir-associated cytopenias (particularly neutropenia), which can complicate early post-engraftment management. If CMV fails to respond or viral load rises above 1,000 IU/mL, we will consider transitioning to IV ganciclovir. Renal function is adequate for current valganciclovir dosing.

4. RESOLVED SOS/VOD — NO ONGOING MANAGEMENT REQUIRED:
Mild SOS that developed around day +12 (peak bilirubin 2.8 mg/dL, peak ALT 142 U/L, meeting Modified Seattle Criteria) has completely resolved; today's LFTs are within normal limits (total bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL, ALT 38 U/L). No ongoing intervention needed. Patient should remain well-hydrated and we will avoid nephrotoxic agents where possible.

5. DISEASE STATUS — COMPLETE REMISSION, MRD NEGATIVE:
Patient remains in documented morphologic and molecular complete remission as of day 28 post-induction bone marrow assessment. The combination of MRD negativity by flow cytometry and undetectable FLT3-ITD PCR at that time point, followed by 99% donor chimerism on day +28, provides high confidence that the graft is establishing control over residual disease. The day +60 bone marrow biopsy (with MRD by flow cytometry, FLT3-ITD PCR, DNMT3A ddPCR, cytogenetics, and chimerism on sorted CD34+ progenitors) will provide formal post-transplant disease reassessment. We expect and hope to confirm durable MRD negativity at that time point.

6. TACROLIMUS MANAGEMENT:
Trough at 9.2 ng/mL — squarely within target. Continue current dosing. Blood pressure is mildly elevated today (138/84 mmHg) which may be partly tacrolimus-related; this is being watched. Magnesium supplementation ongoing for tacrolimus-associated hypomagnesemia (Mg 1.7 mg/dL today). Renal function stable. No signs of tacrolimus neurotoxicity. Tacrolimus taper is not planned until at least day +90 in the MUD setting.

7. FUNCTIONAL STATUS AND NUTRITION:
ECOG PS 1 at this visit, a significant improvement from the peri-transplant period. Patient is eating and drinking adequately, and no parenteral nutrition is required. Weight remains below her pre-AML baseline (61.2 vs. 67 kg), with gradual recovery anticipated over the coming months. She has been referred to our outpatient BMT dietitian. Light physical activity and gradual reconditioning encouraged.

8. DIABETES MANAGEMENT:
Metformin remains held post-transplant pending renal function stabilization and resolution of the acute illness period. Will plan to resume metformin when renal function is confirmed stable and oral intake is reliable (target: resume around day +90 per endocrinology recommendation). Current fingerstick glucose 118 mg/dL — acceptable. Tacrolimus-related insulin resistance acknowledged; if glycemic control deteriorates, endocrinology to be reconsulted.

9. PROPHYLAXIS MEDICATIONS:
All prophylactic medications continued as per transplant protocol: trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for PCP prophylaxis, acyclovir for HSV/VZV prophylaxis (note: valganciclovir provides coverage for CMV as well as some HSV/VZV activity, but we are maintaining acyclovir as a backstop), and fluconazole for antifungal prophylaxis. Aspergillus galactomannan and beta-D-glucan are negative, no clinical concern for invasive fungal infection at this time.

10. PSYCHOSOCIAL:
Patient understandably anxious about relapse risk, which is an expected emotional response in high-risk AML survivors post-transplant. She has been engaged with our BMT social worker and has been finding this helpful. We have reiterated that her initial responses — achieving CR1 MRD-negative and 99% donor chimerism at day +28 — are highly favorable prognostic signs. We encouraged her to continue the counseling support and to reach out to our support resources.

FOLLOW-UP PLAN:
- Return to BMT clinic in 1 week (day +37) for CBC, CMP, tacrolimus trough, and CMV PCR result review
- CMV PCR result from today to be called to patient when resulted; if rising or >1,000 IU/mL, call patient to clinic sooner
- Bone marrow biopsy scheduling: Day +60 (+/- 5 days) — MRD by flow cytometry, FLT3-ITD PCR, DNMT3A R882H ddPCR, cytogenetics, chimerism on sorted lineages
- Peripheral blood chimerism: Repeat at day +60
- Continue all current prophylactic medications
- Continue tacrolimus at current dose and frequency; adjust based on trough levels
- Dietitian referral active
- BMT social worker follow-up ongoing
- Patient instructed to monitor temperature twice daily and call immediately for fever ≥38.0°C, new rash, increasing diarrhea, or any other concerning symptoms
- Emergency BMT on-call line number confirmed with patient
